## SELF-SUPERVISED VARIATIONAL AUTO-ENCODERS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

intelligence. Variational Auto-Encoders (VAEs) constitute a single framework to achieve these goals. Here, we present a novel class of generative models, called _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoder_ (selfVAE), that utilizes deterministic and discrete transformations of data. This class of models allows performing both conditional and unconditional sampling while simplifying the objective function. First, we use a single self-supervised transformation as a latent variable, where a transformation is either downscaling or edge detection. Next, we consider a hierarchical architecture, i.e., multiple transformations, and we show its benefits compared to the VAE. The flexibility of selfVAE in data reconstruction finds a particularly interesting use case in data compression tasks, where we can trade-off memory for better data quality, and vice-versa. We present the performance of our approach on three benchmark image data (Cifar10, Imagenette64, and CelebA).

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The framework of variational autoencoders (VAEs) provides a principled approach for learning latentvariable models. As it utilizes a meaningful low-dimensional latent space with density estimation capabilities, it forms an attractive solution for generative modelling tasks. However, its performance in terms of the test log-likelihood and quality of generated samples is often disappointing, thus, many modifications were proposed. In general, one can obtain a tighter lower bound, and, thus, a more powerful and flexible model, by advancing over the following three components: the _encoder_ (Rezende et al., 2014; van den Berg et al., 2018; Hoogeboom et al., 2020; Maaløe et al., 2016), the _prior_ (or _marginal_ over latents) (Chen et al., 2016; Habibian et al., 2019; Lavda et al., 2020; Lin & Clark, 2020; Tomczak & Welling, 2017) and the _decoder_ (Gulrajani et al., 2016). Recent studies have shown that by employing deep hierarchical architectures and by carefully designing building blocks of the neural networks, VAEs can successfully model high-dimensional data and reach state-of-the-art test likelihoods (Zhao et al., 2017; Maaløe et al., 2019; Vahdat & Kautz, 2020).

In this work, we present a novel class of VAEs, called _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoders_ , where we introduce additional variables to VAEs that result from discrete and deterministic transformations of observed images. Since the transformations are deterministic, and they provide a specific aspect of images (e.g., contextual information through detecting edges or downscaling), we refer to them as _self-supervised representations_ . The introduction of the discrete and deterministic variables allows to train deep hierarchical models efficiently by decomposing the task of learning a highly complex distribution into training smaller and conditional distributions. In this way, the model allows to integrate the prior knowledge about the data, but still enables to synthesize unconditional samples. Furthermore, the discrete and deterministic variables could be used to conditionally reconstruct data, which could be of great use in data compression and super-resolution tasks.

We make the following contributions: i) We propose an extension of the VAE framework by incorporating self-supervised representations of the data. ii) We analyze the impact of modelling natural images with different data transformations as self-supervised representations. iii) This new type of generative model ( _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoders_ ), which is able to perform both conditional and unconditional sampling, demonstrate improved quantitative performance in terms of density estimation and generative capabilities on image benchmarks.

The expectation is approximated by Monte Carlo sampling while exploiting the _reparameterization trick_ in order to obtain unbiased gradient estimators. The models are parameterized by neural networks. This generative framework is known as _Variational Auto-Encoder_ (VAE) (Kingma & Welling, 2013; Rezende et al., 2014).

## 2.2 VAES WITH BIJECTIVE PRIORS

Even though the lower-bound suggests that the prior plays a crucial role in improving the variational bounds, usually a fixed distribution is used, e.g., a standard multivariate Gaussian. While being relatively simple and computationally cheap, the fixed prior is known to result in over-regularized models that tend to ignore most of the latent dimensions (Burda et al., 2015; Hoffman & Johnson, 2016; Tomczak & Welling, 2017). Moreover, even with powerful encoders, VAEs may still fail to match the variational posterior to a unit Gaussian prior (Rosca et al., 2018).

However, it is possible to obtain a rich, multi-modal prior distribution _p_ ( **z** ) by using a _bijective_ (or _flow-based_ ) model (Dinh et al., 2016). ## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 MOTIVATION

The idea of self-supervised learning is about utilizing original unlabeled data to create additional context information. It could be achieved in multiple manners, e.g., by adding noise to data (Vincent et al., 2008) or masking data during training (Zhang et al., 2017). Self-supervised learning could also be seen as turning an unsupervised model into a supervised by, e.g., treating predicting next pixels as a classification task (Hénaff et al., 2019; Oord et al., 2018). These are only a few examples of a quickly growing research line (Liu et al., 2020).

Here, we propose to use non-trainable transformations to obtain information about image data. Our main hypothesis is that since working with highly-quality images is challenging, we could alleviate this problem by additionally considering partial information about them. Fitting a model to images of lower quality, and then enhancing them to match the target distribution seems to be overall an easier task (Chang et al., 2004; Gatopoulos et al., 2020). By incorporating compressed transformations (i.e., the self-supervised representations) that still contain global information, with the premise that it would be easier to approximate, the process of modelling a high-dimensional complex density breaks down into simpler tasks. In this way, the expressivity of the model will grow and gradually result into richer, better generations. A positive effect of the proposed framework is that the model allows us to integrate prior knowledge through the image transformations, without losing its uncon-

## 3.2 MODEL FORMULATION

In our model, we consider representations that result from _deterministic_ and _discrete_ transformations of an image. Formally, we introduce a transformation _d_ : _X[D] ! X[C]_ that takes **x** and returns an image representation **y** , e.g., a downscaled image. Since we lose information about the original image, **z** could be seen as a variable that compensates lost details in **x** . Further we propose to introduce an additional latent variable, **u** _2_ R _[N]_ to model **y** and **z** . We can define the joint distribution of **x** and **y** as follows: _p_ ( **x** _,_ **y** ) = _p_ ( **y** _|_ **x** ) _p_ ( **x** ), where _p_ ( **y** _|_ **x** ) = _δ_ ( **y** _−d_ ( **x** )) due to the deterministic transformation _d_ ( _·_ ), where _δ_ ( _·_ ) is the Kronecker delta. Thus, the empirical distribution is _δ_ ( **y** _− d_ ( **x** )) _pdata_ ( **x** ). Intuitively, the premise for selfVAE is that the latents **u** will capture the global structure of the input data and the latents **z** will encode the missing information between **y** and **x** , guiding the model to discover the distribution of the target observations. In order to highlight the self-supervised part in our model, we refer to it as the _self-supervised Variational Auto-Encoder_ (or selfVAE for short).

Notice that we use the discretized logistic distribution, because images are represented by values between 0 and 255. For integer-valued random variables, other distributions like Gaussian are inappropriate.

The presented versions of generating and reconstructing images could be useful in the compression task. However, every inferred variable needs to be sent, thus, more sampling corresponds to lower memory requirements.

## 3.4 HIERARCHICAL SELF-SUPERVISED VAE

The proposed approach can be further extended and generalized by introducing multiple transformations, in the way that it is illustrated in Figure 3. By incorporating a single (or multiple) self-supervised representation(s) of the data, the process of modelling a high-dimensional complex density breaks down into _K_ simpler modeling tasks. Thus, we obtain a _K_ -level VAE architecture, where the overall expressivity of the model grows even further and gradually results into generations of higher quality. Some transformations cannot be applied multiple times (e.g., edge detection), however, others could be used sequentially, e.g., downscaling.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

**Datasets** We evaluate the proposed model on CIFAR-10, Imagenette64 and CelebA:

_CIFAR-10_ The CIFAR-10 dataset is a well-known image benchmark data containing 60.000 training examples and 10.000 validation examples. From the training data, we put aside 15% randomly selected images as the test set. We augment the training data by using random horizontal flips and random affine transformations and normalize the data uniformly in the range (0, 1).

_Imagenette64_ Imagenette64[2] is a subset of 10 classes from the downscaled Imagenet dataset. We downscaled the dataset to 64px _⇥_ 64px images. Similarly to CIFAR-10, we put aside 15% randomly selected training images as the test set. We used the same data augmentation as in CIFAR-10

_CelebA_ The Large-scale CelebFaces Attributes (CelebA) Dataset consists of 202 _._ 599 images of celebrities. We cropped original images on the 40 vertical and 15 horizontal component of the top left corner of the crop box, which height and width were cropped to 148. Besides the uniform normalization of the image, no other augmentation was applied.

**Architectures** Encoders and decoders consist of building blocks composed of DenseNets (Huang et al., 2016), channel-wise attention (Zhang et al., 2018), and ELUs (Clevert et al., 2015) as activation functions. The dimensionality of all the latent variables were kept at 8 _⇥_ 8 _⇥_ 16 = 1024 and all models were trained using AdaMax (Kingma & Ba, 2014) with data-dependent initialization (Salimans & Kingma, 2016). Regarding the selfVAEs, in CIFAR-10 we used an architecture with a single downscaled transformation (selfVAE-downscale), while on the remaining two datasets (CelebA

## 4.2 QUANTITATIVE RESULTS

We present the results of the experiments on the benchmark datasets in Table 1. First, we notice that on CIFAR-10 our implementation of the VAE is still lacking behind other generative models in terms of _bpd_ , however, it is better or comparable in terms of FID. The selfVAE-downscale achieves worse _bpd_ than the VAE. A possible explanation may lie in the small image size (32 _⇥_ 32), as the benefits of breaking down the learning process in two or more steps are not obvious given the small target dimensional space. Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models. This result could follow from the fact that downscaling allows to maintain context information about the original image and, as a result, a general coherence is of higher quality.

_bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior. This indicates the benefit of employing a multileveled self-supervised framework against the VAE in higher-dimensional data, where the plain model fails to scale efficiently. It seems that the hierarchical structure of self-supervised random variables allows to encode the missing information more efficiently in **z** _k_ , in contrast to the vanilla VAE, where all information about images must be coded in **z** . This result is promising and indicates that the proposed approach is of great potential for generative modelling.

from VAE that produces overall more contextless and distorted generations. This result seems to be in line with the FID scores. Especially for CelebA, we observe impressive synthesis quality, great sampling diversity and coherent generations (Figure 5). On the Imagenette64 dataset, we can also observe crisper generations for our method compared to the VAE (Figure 4).

Furthermore, the hierarchical selfVAE seems to be of a great potential for compression purposes. In contrast to the VAE, which is restricted to using a single way of reconstructing an image, the selfVAE allows four various options with different quality/memory ratios (Figure 6). In the selfVAE-sketch, we can retrieve the image with high accuracy by using only 16% of the original data, as it manages to encode all the texture of the image to **z** (Figure 11). This shows the advantage of choosing prior knowledge into the learning process. Lastly, the latents learn to add extra information, which defines the end result, and we can alter details of an image like facial expressions (Figure 12.ii).

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we showed that taking deterministic and discrete transformations results in coherent generations of high visual quality, and allows to integrate prior knowledge without loosing its unconditional generative functionality. The experimental results seem to confirm that hierarchical architectures perform better and allow to obtain both better _bpd_ scores and better generations and reconstructions. In the experiments, we considered two classes of image transformations, namely, _downscaling_ and edge detection ( _sketching_ ). However, there is a vast of possible other transformations (see Figure 2), and we leave investigating them for future work. Moreover, we find the proposed approach interesting for the compression task. A similar approach with a multi-scale auto-encoder for image compression was proposed, e.g, by Mentzer et al. (2019) or Razavi et al. (2019). However, we still use a probabilistic framework and indicate that various non-trainable image transformations (not only multiple scales) could be of great potential.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a novel extension of Variational Auto-Encoders called self-supervised VAE (selfVAE), which incorporates deterministic, discrete transformations—such as downscaling and edge detection—as self-supervised representations into the generative modeling framework. The core idea of leveraging fixed, non-trainable image transformations to guide hierarchical modeling is conceptually interesting and aligns well with recent trends in self-supervised and structured generative modeling. The motivation is reasonably clear: by decomposing complex data distributions into conditional modeling tasks based on transformed views of the data, the model can better capture multi-scale structure while enabling both conditional and unconditional generation. However, the presentation suffers from several critical shortcomings that affect clarity and scientific rigor. Most notably, the technical description of the method is incomplete and ambiguous—particularly in Section 3.2 (missing in the provided text) and beyond—leaving unclear how exactly the self-supervised transformations are integrated into the VAE objective, how they interact with the latent variables, and how the hierarchical architecture is constructed. The mathematical formulation abruptly cuts off mid-sentence, undermining confidence in the methodological contribution. Furthermore, while the use of bijective priors is mentioned, its connection to the main proposal is under-explained, and no ablation study is visible in the submitted portion to substantiate the claimed improvements. The paper also risks overstating novelty, as conditioning VAEs on engineered features or multi-scale transformations has precedents in hierarchical and conditional VAEs, image-to-image translation, and codec-based generative models. To strengthen the work, the authors must clearly formalize their model's objective, clarify the role of deterministic transformations in the ELBO, and provide a more thorough comparison to related approaches in both method design and experimental setup. As it stands, the idea shows promise but lacks sufficient technical depth and exposition for full evaluation.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed model performance and results in Table 1 and text

comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction claim broad 'improved quantitative performance', but Table 1 shows selfVAE-downscale underperforms the baseline VAE on CIFAR-10 (3.65 vs 3.51 bpd) and selfVAE-sketch underperforms on CelebA (3.24 vs 3.12). Additionally, the text asserts selfVAE has 'significantly better FID scores than other generative models', but DDPM achieves 5.24 FID (test) vs selfVAE's 34.71, invalidating the comparison. These overclaims misrepresent the results, particularly as bpd and FID are central evaluation metrics.

Furthermore, the conditional p(**y**|**u**) and p(**z**|**u**, **y**) are not defined until the next section. This creates ambiguity: is **u** a latent variable with a learnable prior, or is it directly parameterized through p(**y**|**u**)? comments.title: Claim of improved quantitative performance contradicted by Table 1

comments.title: Undefined relationship between transformation function d and representation y_k in hierarchical model
comments.quote: We take _K_ self-supervised data transformations _dk_ ( _·_ ) that give _K_ representations denoted by **y** 1: _K_ = [ **y** 1 _, . . . ,_ **y** _K_ ]
comments.explanation: The text introduces multiple transformations dk but does not clarify their structure: are they applied sequentially (e.g., downscale → downscale again), in parallel (downscale, sketch, etc.), or recursively? Figure 3 shows a layered structure, suggesting sequential processing: **x** → **y_1** via d1, then **y_1** → **y_2** via d2, etc. However, this contradicts the use of edge detection (non-reversible, structure-losing) in sequence, as the authors note 'some transformations cannot be applied multiple times'. Yet no guidance is given on how to compose K levels appropriately. Moreover, the role of each **y_k** in the generative and inference graphs is not formally specified beyond the figure. For example, does p(**x**|**y_1:K**, **z_1:K**) depend on all y_k? Or is each z_k tied to a single y_k? Without this, the model is underspecified.

comments.title: Ambiguity in interpretation of deterministic transformation variable **y** in joint distribution
comments.quote: We can define the joint distribution of **x** and **y** as follows: _p_ ( **x** _,_ **y** ) = _p_ ( **y** _|_ **x** ) _p_ ( **x** ), where _p_ ( **y** _|_ **x** ) = _δ_ ( **y** _−d_ ( **x** )) due to the deterministic transformation _d_ ( _·_ ), where _δ_ ( _·_ ) is the Kronecker delta.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the use of the Kronecker delta suggests that **y** is a discrete variable, which aligns with the paper's emphasis on *discrete* transformations. However, since **y** = d(**x**) is an image transformation (e.g., downscaling or sketching), it may result in a discrete-valued but high-dimensional object, and using δ(**y** − d(**x**)) implies a point mass at the exact transformed value. But if **y** lives on a discrete grid (e.g., integer pixel intensities), the Kronecker delta is appropriate. The concern was whether this leads to issues in likelihood evaluation or measure-theoretic consistency when **y** is both deterministic and high-dimensional. However, the context clarifies that the transformations are deterministic and discrete, and the use of δ is standard for representing such degenerate conditional distributions. The empirical distribution is also correctly formulated as δ(**y** − d(**x**)) p_data(**x**). Still, the paper does not clarify how **y** is treated in practice during sampling or training—since it's not sampled but computed exactly, this raises a subtle ambiguity about whether the model truly conditions on a random variable or on a deterministic function. This matters because in variational inference, treating a deterministic quantity as a random variable in the graphical model can be misleading. While technically sound in spirit, the presentation risks implying that **y** is a latent variable, when it is in fact observed (and deterministic). This could mislead a reader about the generative process. A Dirac delta (for continuous spaces) might have been more technically precise if the transformation were real-valued, but since pixel values are discrete, Kronecker delta is acceptable. Still, the lack of clarification between deterministic input and latent status of **y** is a subtle but notable ambiguity.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed number of latent variables and formal definition
comments.quote: Overall, we end up with a two-level VAE with three latent variables, where one is a data transformation that can be obtained in a self-supervised fashion.
comments.explanation: The paper states there are 'three latent variables', including the self-supervised representation **y** = d(**x**). However, in standard terminology, a 'latent variable' is a random variable not observed, whereas **y** is a deterministic function of **x**, hence fully observed and not latent in the probabilistic sense. While the paper formally treats **y** as a variable in the joint distribution _p_(**x**, **y**), it is degenerate—it has no entropy and is not inferred but computed. Calling it a 'latent variable' is therefore misleading and inconsistent with usual VAE terminology. The other two stochastic variables are **u** and **z**, so there are only two proper latent variables. This mislabeling could cause confusion, especially for readers trying to map the model to standard hierarchical VAEs. The intent—to treat **y** as a structured auxiliary input in the latent space hierarchy—is clear and reasonable, but referring to **y** as a latent variable blurs the line between auxiliary input and latent stochastic node. Given the paper already defines self-supervised representations as deterministic, the claim of 'three latent variables' contradicts its own definitions. This is not a technical flaw in the math, but a logical overstatement that could hinder clarity.

comments.title: Missing normalization constant in discretized logistic distribution
comments.quote: where Dlogistic is defined as the discretized logistic distribution (Salimans et al., 2017), and we utilize a flow-based model for _pλ_ ( **u** ).
comments.explanation: The passage mentions the use of the discretized logistic distribution for modeling **x** conditioned on (**y**, **z**), which is standard practice for modeling 8-bit image data in likelihood-based models. However, in the ELBO derivation (Equation 4), the term _p_θ(**x**|**y**, **z**) is used without any mention of the discretization correction (i.e., the +1/256 logistic CDF difference). The reference to Salimans et al. (2017) implies proper discretization is applied, but the equation itself does not reflect this. In particular, for integer-valued outputs, _p_θ(**x**|**y**, **z**) should involve integrating the continuous logistic density over bins [**x**−0.5, **x**+0.5] to avoid infinite likelihoods. The omission from the equation is not a fatal error given the citation, but since the paper emphasizes 'because images are represented by values between 0 and 255', it would be expected to at least note that the likelihood includes the discretization term. This is especially important because using a continuous distribution (e.g., Gaussian) without adjustment would indeed be inappropriate. While context suggests correct implementation, the formal objective (Eq. 4) is underspecified in this regard. It's a moderate concern because a reader trying to reproduce the method might miss this detail despite the citation.

comments.title: Ambiguous variational posterior in hierarchical selfVAE
comments.quote: We take _K_ self-supervised data transformations _dk_ ( _·_ ) that give _K_ representations denoted by **y** 1: _K_ = [ **y** 1 _, . . . ,_ **y** _K_ ], and the following variational distributions:
comments.explanation: The sentence sets up the hierarchical extension but abruptly ends without specifying the variational distributions. While the full paper likely defines them in a following equation (not included in the context), as presented here, this is a severe underspecification. The non-hierarchical version uses _Q_(**u**, **z**|**x**, **y**) = _q_(**u**|**y**) _q_(**z**|**x**), which is mean-field across **u** and **z** but conditions each on different inputs (**y** and **x**, respectively). In the K-level case, it's unclear how the inference model scales: does each **u**_k depend only on **y**_k? Is **z**_k inferred from **x** or from **y**_{k+1}? Are the **y**_k deterministic transformations at different scales (e.g., x4, x3, x2)? The paper says only that multiple transformations are used, but no structure is given. For reproducibility, the form of the approximate posterior is essential. While Figure 3 suggests a cascade, without explicit definition, it's impossible to know whether the inference model is _q_(**u**_1:K_, **z**_1:K_ | **x**, **y**_1:K_) and how factorization is assumed. This is not just missing text—it's a critical omission in the method description. Even if later sections fill it in, the formulation section as presented is incomplete. Given the claim of improved performance hinges on the hierarchical design, this lack of specification is problematic.

comments.title: Conditional generation and reconstruction processes lack formal alignment with ELBO
comments.quote:
Then, the generative process for the former ( _conditional generation_ ) is:

**z** _⇠ q_ ( **z** _|_ **x** _[⇤]_ ) _−!_ **x** _⇠ p_ ( **x** _|_ **y** _[⇤] ,_ **z** ) _,_

and for the latter ( _conditional reconstruction_ ):
comments.explanation: The paper presents several sampling paths—conditional generation, reconstruction 1, reconstruction 2—without explaining how these relate to the training objective or the ELBO. In particular, conditional generation uses the encoder **z** ~ q(**z**|**x**) but conditions the decoder on **y**[⇤], which is consistent. However, reconstruction 1 and 2 involve **u** ~ q(**u**|**y**[⇤]), which is the encoder for **u**, followed by **y** ~ p(**y**|**u**), which is a generative step. This creates a mismatch: the reconstructed **y** may differ from **y**[⇤], yet the model proceeds to sample **z** based on (**u**, **y**) to generate **x**. This is valid as a reconstruction path, but the paper does not clarify that this introduces extra stochasticity. Crucially, these paths are not directly supported by the ELBO, which lower-bounds log p(**x**, **y**), not log p(**x**) alone. The model was trained to reconstruct the pair (**x**, **y**), but at test time it may reconstruct from a perturbed **y**. This is not incorrect per se, but the exposition implies all these modes are equally valid without discussing trade-offs in fidelity or likelihood. Moreover, the ELBO (Eq. 4) includes log p(**y**|**u**) and log p(**u**), which suggests **y** is modeled as stochastic, unlike its role in the deterministic transformation **y** = d(**x**). This creates tension: during training, **y** is fixed by d(**x**), but during generation, **y** is sampled. The model thus treats **y** as both deterministic and stochastic depending on context. While this is common in conditional VAEs, the paper does not explicitly resolve this duality. It’s a subtle but important point: if **y** is observed during training, p(**y**) should not be included in the ELBO unless it's being modeled as a random quantity. The current formulation risks double-counting **y**—once as deterministic input, once as stochastic output.

comments.title: Inconsistency in handling of **y** as both observed and generated variable
comments.explanation: The paper first defines the joint _p_(**x**, **y**) using a delta function to enforce **y** = d(**x**), which makes _p_(**y**|**x**) deterministic. Then it says they model _p_(**x**|**y**) _p_(**y**), which implies a generative process where **y** is sampled from a prior _p_(**y**). But according to the delta definition, _p_(**y**) = ∫ _p_(**y**|**x**) _p_(**x**) d**x** = 𝔼[_δ_**(y** - d(**x**))], which is just the density of the transformed data. The model does not assume a parametric prior over **y**; instead, it learns _p_(**y**|**u**) and _p_(**u**), so effectively _p_(**y**) is induced through **u**. This is valid, but the switch from observing **y** as d(**x**) to generating **y** from _p_(**y**) is not clearly reconciled. In particular, during training, **y** is fixed, so the term log _p_(**y**) in the ELBO (Eq. 4) is modeling how well the model explains the self-supervised representation, which is reasonable. But since **y** is fully determined by **x**, maximizing log _p_(**y**) is equivalent to modeling the distribution of d(**x**) under the assumed prior, which may not be flexible enough. The real issue is conceptual: the model assumes **y** is both a deterministic function of **x** and a random variable with its own prior. While this is manageable in practice (similar to VAEs with observed discrete labels), it introduces a mismatch in the assumed data-generating process. The formulation would be more coherent if **y** were treated solely as an observed auxiliary input, not as a modeled random variable. Including _p_(**y**) in the ELBO suggests the model is density-modeling **y**, but since **y** is redundant given **x**, this may not be necessary and could distort the objective. The paper does not justify why _p_(**y**) is included instead of conditioning on **y** throughout. This affects the interpretation of the ELBO and the model's probabilistic coherence.

comments.title: Mismatch in Objective Derivation for Hierarchical Model
comments.quote: that yields the following objective:
comments.explanation: The passage ends with the phrase 'that yields the following objective:' but provides no actual objective equation. In the hierarchical model (Section 3.4), the variational lower bound must account for K levels of self-supervised representations and their associated latents (**u**_k, **y**_k, **z**_k). From earlier in the paper (Equation 4), we see that with a single **y**, the ELBO includes terms for **u**, **y**, **z**, and **x** with a specific variational factorization Q(**u**, **z**|**x**, **y**) = q(**u**|**y**) q(**z**|**x**). For the K-level case, the objective should generalize this structure by summing over k = 1 to K, with terms like log p(**y**_k|**u**_k), log p(**z**_k|**u**_k, **y**_k), log p(**u**_k), and variational terms log q(**u**_k|**y**_k), log q(**z**_k|**x**_k or **y**_{k−1}), and the reconstruction log p(**x**|**z**_1, ..., **z**_K, **y**_1, ..., **y**_K). However, no such objective is written after 'that yields the following objective:', nor is it referenced in the provided context. This omission occurs right after introducing the factorization of variational distributions via Figure 3. While the single-level case (Eq. 4) provides a plausible template, the multi-level dependency structure (e.g., whether **z**_k depends on all previous **y** or only **y**_k) is not formally specified. The lack of the actual formula forces the reader to guess the model's mathematical formulation, which is problematic for reproducibility. Although the prose says the density modeling breaks into K simpler tasks, it does not specify how the likelihood or prior terms are factored across levels. Given the paper's emphasis on precise variational bounds and bijective priors, this omission is significant.

comments.explanation: In Figure 3, each level appears to have both **u** and **y** at each level k, with transformations d applied from **y**_k to **x** or lower **y**_{k−1}. However, the figure layout is ambiguous. The 'u u' at the top suggests a single shared **u**, but the replication of 'u' at each level (implied by the parallel 'u u' and vertical structure) suggests multiple **u**_k, one per level. This contradicts Section 3.2, where **u** ∈ ℝᴺ is introduced as a single additional latent for modeling **y**, and **y** is derived from **x** via d(**x**). In the hierarchical extension (Section 3.4), it states: 'We take K self-supervised data transformations d_k(·) that give K representations denoted by **y**_1:K = [**y**_1, ..., **y**_K]'. But it does not explicitly say whether there is a single **u** or multiple **u**_k. The variational distributions mentioned right after Figure 3 are described as 'that yields the following objective'—but no equation is shown. In the inference model (b), we see chains like **u** → **y**_k → **z**_k, repeated for each k, suggesting independent inference paths per level, which would require a separate **u**_k per level. However, this would contradict the earlier single **u** definition. The model summary states **u** ∈ ℝᴺ as a single variable. Thus, unless **u** is reused or shared across levels (which should be clarified), the diagram suggests a multi-**u** structure not supported by the text. Given that **u** is used to model **y**, and **y** now has K components, it is likely the authors intend **u**_k per level. But without explicit definition or objective, this remains ambiguous. OCR is unlikely to cause this, as 'u' appears consistently. The figure may intend to show a hierarchical cascade but fails to annotate variables per level clearly.

comments.title: Unclear Dependency Structure in Hierarchical Generative Process
comments.quote: a) Generative Model b) Inference Model
comments.explanation: In Figure 3(a), the generative model shows arrows from **u** to **y**_K, **y**_K to **z**_K, and **z**_K to **x**, but also from **y**_{K−1} to **z**_{K−1}, etc., down to **z**_1 connected to **x**. However, the exact generative factorization is unclear. Is the full image **x** generated from all **z**_k and **y**_k? Or is there a cascade: **x** generated from **z**_1 and **y**_1, with **y**_1 generated from **z**_2 and **y**_2, and so on? The figure layout with multiple **z**_k and **y**_k all pointing to **x** suggests that **x** is generated conditionally on all latent variables, i.e., p(**x** | **y**_1:K, **z**_1:K). Alternatively, it might represent a recursive upsampling process where **y**_{k-1} is generated from **y**_k and **z**_k, ultimately leading to **x** from **y**_1 and **z**_1. But in that case, **x** should be at the end of a chain, not receiving input from all **z**_k directly. The diagram lacks directional clarity: the 'd' annotations suggest deterministic downsampling, but in the generative direction, we need upsampling. The inference model (b) shows **x** → **z**_1 → **y**_1 → **z**_2 → ..., which suggests a bottom-up inference path, but again, **y**_k is fed into **z**_k, which is inconsistent with **z** encoding residuals from **y**_k to **x**. Given that **y**_k = d_k(**y**_{k-1}) (e.g., repeated downscaling), the inference model should infer **z**_k to recover detail lost from **y**_k to **y**_{k-1} (or **x**). But the current diagram suggests **z**_k is generated from **y**_k, which would make it depend on the downsampled version, not help reconstruct it. The lack of a formal generative factorization (e.g., p(**u**_1:K) ∏_k p(**y**_k|**u**_k) p(**z**_k|**u**_k, **y**_k) p(**x**|**z**_1:K, **y**_1:K)) or inference factorization leaves the model underspecified. This ambiguity affects interpretation of how information flows and how losses are computed.

comments.title: Inconsistency Between Stated Architecture and Figure 6 Labels
comments.quote:
VAE selfVAE-downscale-2lvl

comments.explanation: In Figure 6, the labels include 'selfVAE-downscale-2lvl', but in the experimental setup (Section 4.1), the authors state: 'in CIFAR-10 we used an architecture with a single downscaled transformation' and 'on the remaining two datasets (CelebA and Imagenette64) we used a hierarchical 3-leveled selfVAE with downscaling, and a selfVAE with sketching'. Nowhere is a 2-level variant mentioned as part of the experiments. Table 1 reports results only for 'selfVAE-downscale' (likely 1-level) and 'selfVAE-downscale-3lvl', with no 2-level variant. Yet Figure 6 includes 'selfVAE-downscale-2lvl' in the legend for the CIFAR-10 reconstructions. This suggests either: (1) a 2-level model was used but not described, (2) it's a typo and should be 'selfVAE-downscale', or (3) the figure includes ablation results not reported in the text or table. Since the paper is under review and all results should be reproducible, introducing an unmentioned model variant in a figure is misleading. It could be an OCR or formatting error (e.g., '−3lvl' misread as '−2lvl'), but the text clearly says '3-leveled' for CelebA and Imagenette64, and only single-level for CIFAR-10. Given that the figure shows 'selfVAE-downscale-2lvl' in the context of CIFAR-10 (which supposedly used only single-level), this is a serious inconsistency. Either the architecture description is incomplete, or the figure label is incorrect.

comments.title: Missing Definition of Multi-Level Transformation Dependencies
comments.quote: We take _K_ self-supervised data transformations _dk_ ( _·_ ) that give _K_ representations denoted by **y** 1: _K_ = [ **y** 1 _, . . . ,_ **y** _K_ ], and the following variational distributions:
comments.explanation: The text says K transformations d_k(·) produce K representations **y**_1:K, but does not specify how these transformations are related. For downscaling, it is natural to assume a cascade: **y**_1 = d_1(**x**), **y**_2 = d_2(**y**_1), ..., **y**_K = d_K(**y**_{K−1}), e.g., repeated 2x downsampling. However, the text does not state this. The joint distribution p(**x**, **y**_1:K) is not defined. In the single-level case, it is p(**x**, **y**) = δ(**y** − d(**x**)) p_data(**x**). In the multi-level case, it should be p(**x**, **y**_1:K) = [∏_{k=1}^K δ(**y**_k − d_k(**y**_{k−1}))] p_data(**x**) with **y**_0 = **x**. But this is not written. Without specifying this deterministic dependency, the model's observational likelihood is underspecified. Furthermore, the variational distribution Q(**u**, **z**|**x**, **y**) in the single level uses q(**u**|**y**) and q(**z**|**x**), but in the K-level case, with multiple **y**_k, it's unclear which **y**_k is used for q(**u**_k|**y**_k), or whether the encoder sees the full cascade. The sentence ends with 'and the following variational distributions:' but no distributions are listed in the provided context—another critical omission. This lack of definition makes it impossible to verify the validity of the implied ELBO.

While Figure 3 graphically shows the inference path (bottom-up, from x through y_k, z_k to u), no explicit specification of Q(**u**, **z**_{1:K} | **x**) is given—e.g., whether it factorizes as q(**u** | **y**_1)∏_k q(**z**_k | **x**, **y**_{≤k}, **u**) or another form. The objective function is mentioned to follow from these distributions, but the objective itself is not written out. This omission makes it impossible to verify the correctness of the ELBO derivation or reproduce the training procedure. comments.title: Ambiguity in reconstruction schemes in Figure 6 and surrounding text
comments.quote:
Figure 6: Comparison on image reconstructions with different amount of sent information.

from VAE that produces overall more contextless and distorted generations. This result seems to be in line with the FID scores. Especially for CelebA, we observe impressive synthesis quality, great sampling diversity and coherent generations (Figure 5). On the Imagenette64 dataset, we can also observe crisper generations for our method compared to the VAE (Figure 4).

Furthermore, the hierarchical selfVAE seems to be of a great potential for compression purposes. In contrast to the VAE, which is restricted to using a single way of reconstructing an image, the selfVAE allows four various options with different quality/memory ratios (Figure 6). In the selfVAE-sketch, we can retrieve the image with high accuracy by using only 16% of the original data, as it manages to encode all the texture of the image to **z** (Figure 11). This shows the advantage of choosing prior knowledge into the learning process. Lastly, the latents learn to add extra information, which defines the end result, and we can alter details of an image like facial expressions (Figure 12.ii).
comments.explanation: The text claims that selfVAE supports 'four various options' for reconstruction, but only three are defined earlier: reconstruction 1, reconstruction 2, and conditional generation. The fourth is presumably 'reconstruction 3', which appears in Figure 6’s legend but is never defined in the text. Readers cannot know what procedure 'reconstruction 3' entails. Additionally, '8%, 8%, 16%, 25%, 33%, 8%, 16%' are listed under the images in Figure 6 with no explanation of what these percentages represent—are they the relative sizes of **y**, **u**, and **z** together? Or just the latent **z**? The paper states that 'every inferred variable needs to be sent', suggesting that both **u** and **z** contribute to the size, but with all latents fixed at 1024 dimensions across models, the compression ratios must derive from how many variables are sampled vs. transmitted. Without clarity on which variables are sent and which are resampled in each scheme, the compression claims are ambiguous. This undermines the interpretability of Figure 6 and the validity of the compression trade-off argument.

comments.title: Inconsistent reporting of FID values in Table 1 and text
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the FID for DDPM is reported as 5.24 (3.17*) with a footnote stating 'Measured on training set'. The selfVAE-downscale achieves 34.71 (29.95*), with asterisk also meaning training set. However, in the main text (Section 4.2), the authors claim: 'the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models'. This claim is incorrect, as DDPM’s test FID is 5.24 and selfVAE-downscale’s test FID is 34.71—over six times worse. Even DDPM's training FID of 3.17 is much better than selfVAE-downscale’s 29.95*. The comparison is not fair, and the overclaim misrepresents the results. Authors may intend to compare against models other than DDPM, but the phrasing 'other generative models' includes DDPM. Given that FID is a key metric for image quality, overstating performance based on partial comparison is misleading.

comments.title: Underspecified compression ratio computation in Figure 6
comments.quote:
8% 8% 16% 25% 33% 8% 16%
VAE selfVAE-downscale-2lvl
VAE selfVAE-downscale-3lvl selfVAE-sketch
Compression

comments.explanation: The percentages (8%, 16%, etc.) in Figure 6 are labeled as 'Compression ratio', but the paper does not define how this ratio is computed. Are these percentages of total data sent relative to the original image size? If so, how are **y**, **u**, and **z** each encoded and sized? While all **z**_k are fixed at 8×8×16 = 1024, **y**_k sizes vary: e.g., a 32×32 image downscaled by 2× becomes 16×16×3 = 768, which is ~24% of the original (32×32×3 = 3072). But this does not align with the reported 8% or 16%. Moreover, how are **u** and **z** quantized or entropy-coded? Without specifying whether the ratios include **y** (which is derived from **x** via deterministic d), or whether they reflect compressed bitrates, the values are uninterpretable. The lack of definition renders the compression efficiency claims in Figures 6 and in the conclusion speculative.

comments.title: Misuse of 'discrete' to describe deterministic transformations
comments.quote: In our model, we consider representations that result from _deterministic_ and _discrete_ transformations of an image.
comments.explanation: The term 'discrete' is used throughout the paper to describe transformations like downscaling and sketching, but in signal processing and machine learning, a 'discrete transformation' typically refers to one that outputs discrete (quantized) values, not just integer-valued pixel arrays. Downscaling via bicubic interpolation (Figure 2.b-d) produces continuous values in [0,1], not discrete ones. Even '1 bit' or 'sketch' yield pixel values in {0,1}, which are discrete, but downscaling does not necessarily do so. The transformation **y** = d(**x**) is deterministic and often lower-resolution, but not inherently discrete unless explicitly quantized. The confusion may stem from OCR or notation, but given consistent usage, it reflects a conceptual looseness. This could mislead readers into thinking the model benefits from discrete latent spaces or quantization. The authors likely mean 'non-learnable', 'fixed', or 'pre-processing', not 'discrete'.

comments.title: Unjustified benefit of hierarchical selfVAE on higher-dimensional data
comments.quote: It seems that the hierarchical structure of self-supervised random variables allows to encode the missing information more efficiently in **z** _k_ , in contrast to the vanilla VAE, where all information about images must be coded in **z** . This result is promising and indicates that the proposed approach is of great potential for generative modelling.
comments.explanation: The claim that hierarchical selfVAE encodes 'missing information more efficiently' is intuitive but not supported by direct evidence. The paper shows improved bpd on CelebA and Imagenette64 with hierarchical versions, but does not ablate whether the gain comes from hierarchy, from the use of **y** as auxiliary input, or from architectural differences. Since all models have comparable parameters, the improvement might stem from better inductive bias rather than efficiency in encoding. Moreover, no analysis (e.g., ablation of levels, z_k utilization, or information allocation) is presented to justify the 'efficiency' claim. While performance gains are demonstrated, the specific mechanism ('more efficient encoding in **z**_k') is speculative without further justification.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents an interesting and timely direction by exploring the integration of deterministic, pre-defined image transformations (e.g., downscaling, edge detection) into the VAE framework, framing them as "self-supervised representations." The core motivation—that modeling a degraded version of an image is an easier sub-problem—is intuitive and potentially powerful for tasks like compression and super-resolution. However, the paper's initial clarity and conceptual framing present significant challenges that must be addressed. The most critical issue is the justification for the term "self-supervised"; the described transformations are non-trainable and deterministic, making the approach appear more akin to conditioning a VAE on a known, derived signal rather than learning representations in the standard self-supervised sense. The authors must more clearly delineate their novelty from standard conditional VAEs. Furthermore, the manuscript currently lacks the necessary mathematical formalism to evaluate its claims, as the introduction of these transformation variables into the ELBO, the resulting graphical model, and the justification for how the objective function is "simplified" are not yet presented. This formal foundation is essential for understanding the technical contribution beyond the high-level concept.

comments.quote: Then, the following objective function could be derived, namely, the evidence lower bound (ELBO) (Jordan et al., 1999):
comments.explanation: The text mentions the ELBO but doesn't actually show the equation. This is a key equation for understanding the VAE framework, and its absence makes it harder for readers to follow the paper. While this appears to be an OCR error, it significantly impacts the clarity of the foundational concepts being presented.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about z compensating for lost details in Section 3.2

As stated, it could be misinterpreted to mean that z compensates for details lost in the original image x, which doesn't make sense since x is the original, complete image.

comments.title: Confusing statement about u modeling y and z in Section 3.2
comments.quote: Further we propose to introduce an additional latent variable, u ∈ R^N to model y and z.
comments.explanation: This statement is confusing. The factorization Q(u, z|x, y) = q(u|y)q(z|x) makes it clear that u is conditioned on y, not z. It would be clearer to say that u is introduced to model y, not y and z.

After stating 'and for the latter (conditional reconstruction):', the authors move on to discuss alternative reconstruction methods without completing the description. Additionally, it's not clear what y is in this case - whether it's the ground-truth y[∗] = d(x[∗]) or a sampled y. This ambiguity should be clarified for readers to understand the reconstruction process.

Without seeing Figure 11 or having a quantitative measure of "high accuracy", it's hard to evaluate this claim. It would be better to provide a more specific measure of accuracy or reference a quantitative result.

comments.title: Misleading claim about simplified objective

comments.explanation: The abstract claims selfVAE 'simplifies the objective function' compared to standard VAEs, but the ELBO presented in Section 3.2 has more terms and involves more variables than the standard VAE ELBO. While the decomposition of the modeling task might simplify the optimization process conceptually, the objective function itself is not simpler in terms of mathematical complexity. This claim is misleading and not substantiated in the paper.

comments.title: Unclear comparison with baselines

The paper doesn't specify whether these results were taken from other papers (potentially with different preprocessing, evaluation protocols, or hyperparameter settings) or if the authors re-implemented all baselines. This ambiguity affects the fairness of the comparison.

progressive_original__glm-4.6.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.6)
progressive_original__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents an interesting and timely direction by exploring the integration of deterministic, pre-defined image transformations (e.g., downscaling, edge detection) into the VAE framework, framing them as "self-supervised representations." The core motivation—that modeling a degraded version of an image is an easier sub-problem—is intuitive and potentially powerful for tasks like compression and super-resolution. However, the paper's initial clarity and conceptual framing present significant challenges that must be addressed. The most critical issue is the justification for the term "self-supervised"; the described transformations are non-trainable and deterministic, making the approach appear more akin to conditioning a VAE on a known, derived signal rather than learning representations in the standard self-supervised sense. The authors must more clearly delineate their novelty from standard conditional VAEs. Furthermore, the manuscript currently lacks the necessary mathematical formalism to evaluate its claims, as the introduction of these transformation variables into the ELBO, the resulting graphical model, and the justification for how the objective function is "simplified" are not yet presented. This formal foundation is essential for understanding the technical contribution beyond the high-level concept.

comments.title: Missing ELBO equation in Section 2.1
comments.quote: Then, the following objective function could be derived, namely, the evidence lower bound (ELBO) (Jordan et al., 1999):
comments.explanation: The text mentions the ELBO but doesn't actually show the equation. This is a key equation for understanding the VAE framework, and its absence makes it harder for readers to follow the paper. While this appears to be an OCR error, it significantly impacts the clarity of the foundational concepts being presented.

comments.title: Garbled change of variables formula in Section 2.2
comments.quote: @fi(v_{i-1})_th where is the Jacobian-determinant of the _i_ transformation.  @_v_{i-1}
comments.explanation: The mathematical notation for the change of variables formula is garbled, likely due to OCR errors. The correct formula would involve the determinant of the Jacobian matrix of the transformation. This is a key equation for understanding the bijective prior, and its garbled form makes it difficult to follow the derivation.

comments.title: Missing hierarchical objective function in Section 3.4
comments.quote: that yields the following objective:
comments.explanation: The text mentions an objective function for the hierarchical selfVAE but doesn't show it. This is a key equation for understanding the extension of the model to multiple transformations. While this appears to be an OCR error, it significantly impacts the clarity of the hierarchical model formulation.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about z compensating for lost details in Section 3.2
comments.quote: Since we lose information about the original image, z could be seen as a variable that compensates lost details in x.
comments.explanation: This statement is ambiguous. It would be clearer to say that z compensates for the lost details in y (the transformed representation), not x. As stated, it could be misinterpreted to mean that z compensates for details lost in the original image x, which doesn't make sense since x is the original, complete image.

comments.title: Confusing statement about u modeling y and z in Section 3.2
comments.quote: Further we propose to introduce an additional latent variable, u ∈ R^N to model y and z.
comments.explanation: This statement is confusing. The factorization Q(u, z|x, y) = q(u|y)q(z|x) makes it clear that u is conditioned on y, not z. It would be clearer to say that u is introduced to model y, not y and z.

comments.title: Ambiguous y in conditional reconstruction process in Section 3.3
comments.quote: and for the latter (conditional reconstruction): z ∼ q(z|x[∗]) → x ∼ p(x|y, z)
comments.explanation: It's not clear what y is in this case. Is it the ground-truth y[∗] = d(x[∗]) or a sampled y? This ambiguity should be clarified for readers to understand the reconstruction process.

comments.title: Strong claim about selfVAE-sketch in Section 4.3 without quantitative support
comments.quote: In the selfVAE-sketch, we can retrieve the image with high accuracy by using only 16% of the original data, as it manages to encode all the texture of the image to z (Figure 11).
comments.explanation: This is a strong claim that would benefit from more quantitative support. Without seeing Figure 11 or having a quantitative measure of "high accuracy", it's hard to evaluate this claim. It would be better to provide a more specific measure of accuracy or reference a quantitative result.

comments.title: Vague reference to result in Section 4.3
comments.quote: This result seems to be in line with the FID scores.
comments.explanation: It's not clear which result this refers to. It would be better to be more specific about which result is being referred to, especially since multiple results are discussed in this section.

comments.title: Missing conditional reconstruction process
comments.quote: However, we can use the ground-truth **y** (i.e, **y** _[⇤]_ = _d_ ( **x** _[⇤]_ )), and sample or infer **z** . Then, the generative process for the former ( _conditional generation_ ) is: **z** _⇠ q_ ( **z** _|_ **x** _[⇤]_ ) _−!_ **x** _⇠ p_ ( **x** _|_ **y** _[⇤] ,_ **z** ) _,_ and for the latter ( _conditional reconstruction_ ):
comments.explanation: The text introduces 'conditional reconstruction' as a distinct process but fails to specify what this process actually entails. After stating 'and for the latter (conditional reconstruction):', the authors move on to discuss alternative reconstruction methods without completing the description of conditional reconstruction. This leaves readers unclear about what distinguishes conditional reconstruction from conditional generation in their framework.

comments.title: Underspecified hierarchical variational distributions
comments.quote: We take _K_ self-supervised data transformations _dk_ ( _·_ ) that give _K_ representations denoted by **y** 1: _K_ = [ **y** 1 _, . . . ,_ **y** _K_ ], and the following variational distributions:
comments.explanation: The authors introduce the hierarchical extension but state 'and the following variational distributions:' without actually specifying what these distributions are. This makes it impossible for readers to understand the exact formulation of the hierarchical model or reproduce it. Given that the hierarchical version is crucial for their claims about improved performance on higher-dimensional data, this omission is significant.

u
y
z
z
d
x

comments.title: Missing objective function equation
comments.quote: that yields the following objective:
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states 'that yields the following objective:' but no equation follows. This appears to be an OCR extraction error where the mathematical equation was not captured. The hierarchical selfVAE objective should be presented here, as it's a key contribution of the paper.

comments.title: Confusing CIFAR-10 test set description
comments.quote: From the training data, we put aside 15% randomly selected images as the test set.
comments.explanation: This is confusing because CIFAR-10 already has a designated test set. The authors seem to be using validation data as test data, which is non-standard. While not necessarily wrong, this should be clarified - are they using the standard test split or creating their own? If the latter, this makes comparison with other papers using the standard split problematic.

comments.title: Unclear CelebA cropping procedure
comments.quote: We cropped original images on the 40 vertical and 15 horizontal component of the top left corner of the crop box, which height and width were cropped to 148.
comments.explanation: This sentence is grammatically awkward and the cropping procedure is unclear. What does '40 vertical and 15 horizontal component' refer to? Is this 40 pixels from top and 15 from left? The description should be rewritten for clarity, as reproducibility depends on precise preprocessing specifications.

comments.title: DDPM FID measured on training set
comments.explanation: The footnote indicates DDPM's FID was measured on training set, which is highly unusual. FID is typically computed between generated samples and real test set images. Measuring on training set would artificially inflate performance. This needs clarification - is this a typo, or did the authors really measure FID on training data?

comments.title: Minor typo in Figure 4 caption
comments.quote: Figure 4: Uncoditional generations on Imagenette64 and CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation: Simple typo: 'Uncoditional' should be 'Unconditional'.

comments.title: Incomplete reference title

comments.explanation: The reference title 'invertible generative modeling' appears to be missing a word at the beginning. Given the authors and year, this is likely referring to 'Neural invertible generative modeling' or similar. While readers familiar with the literature might identify the correct paper, the incomplete title could cause confusion.

comments.title: Author name typo in reference
comments.quote: Olivier J Hénaff, Aravind Srinivas, Jeffrey De Fauw, Ali Razavi, Carl Doorsch, SM Eslami, and Aaron van den Oord.
comments.explanation: The name 'Carl Doorsch' appears to be a typo for 'Carl Doersch', who is a known researcher in this field. This is a minor typographical error that doesn't affect the paper's technical content but should be corrected for accuracy.

comments.title: Missing training hyperparameters
