comments.explanation: The paper mentions the optimizer (AdaMax) and initialization method, but doesn't specify critical hyperparameters like learning rates, batch sizes, or training duration. This makes it difficult for researchers to reproduce the results. While they reference an appendix for more details, the core experimental setup lacks sufficient information in the main text.

comments.title: Overstated claim about improved density estimation
comments.quote: demonstrate improved quantitative performance in terms of density estimation and generative capabilities on image benchmarks.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims selfVAE demonstrates improved density estimation, but Table 1 shows selfVAE-downscale achieves worse bpd (3.65) than the VAE baseline (3.51) on CIFAR-10. While Section 4.2 acknowledges this limitation and suggests it's due to small image size, the abstract claim is misleading as it doesn't hold across all benchmarks.

comments.title: Ambiguous compression ratios
comments.explanation: Figure 6 shows compression ratios (8%, 16%, etc.) without clearly defining what these percentages represent. The text mentions 'every inferred variable needs to be sent, thus, more sampling corresponds to lower memory requirements,' but doesn't explain how the percentages are calculated - whether they refer to percentage of original data, percentage of latent dimensions transmitted, or some other metric. This ambiguity makes it difficult to understand the practical implications of different reconstruction modes.

comments.title: Missing details on sketch transformation
comments.quote: selfVAE-sketch: selfVAE variant using sketching (edge detection) transformation
comments.explanation: The paper mentions using a sketch or edge detection transformation but doesn't provide details about the specific algorithm or parameters used (e.g., Canny, Sobel, threshold values). This makes it difficult for readers to understand exactly what transformation is being applied or to reproduce the experiments. Given that sketching is one of the two main transformations studied, this is a significant omission.

comments.title: Missing objective function for hierarchical model
comments.quote: that yields the following objective:
comments.explanation: In Section 3.4, the authors introduce the hierarchical structure with K transformations but don't provide the actual objective function. The text says 'that yields the following objective:' but doesn't show the resulting ELBO for the K-level case. This omission makes it difficult for readers to understand the exact objective being optimized and how it differs from the two-level case presented earlier.

comments.title: Unclear evaluation protocol
comments.quote: We approximate the negative log-likelihood using 512 IW-samples (Burda et al., 2015)
comments.explanation: The authors mention using 512 IW-samples for approximating the negative log-likelihood but don't specify important details about the evaluation protocol. For example, they don't explain how these samples were generated, whether any variance reduction techniques were used, or how the IW samples were distributed across the hierarchical latents in the multi-level models. This lack of detail makes it difficult to assess the reliability of the likelihood estimates.

comments.title: Misleading claim about simplified objective
comments.quote: This class of models allows performing both conditional and unconditional sampling while simplifying the objective function.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims selfVAE 'simplifies the objective function' compared to standard VAEs, but the ELBO presented in Section 3.2 has more terms and involves more variables than the standard VAE ELBO. While the decomposition of the modeling task might simplify the optimization process conceptually, the objective function itself is not simpler in terms of mathematical complexity. This claim is misleading and not substantiated in the paper.

comments.title: Unclear comparison with baselines
comments.quote: Table 1: Quantitative comparison on test sets from CIFAR-10, CelebA, and Imagenette64.
comments.explanation: Table 1 compares selfVAE with various baselines (PixelCNN, GLOW, ResidualFlow, BIVA, NVAE, DDPM), but it's unclear if these baselines were evaluated under identical conditions. The paper doesn't specify whether these results were taken from other papers (potentially with different preprocessing, evaluation protocols, or hyperparameter settings) or if the authors re-implemented all baselines. This ambiguity affects the fairness of the comparison.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an interesting conceptual framework, "selfVAE," which attempts to bridge self-supervised learning with variational inference by incorporating deterministic, task-specific image transformations into the generative process. The motivation is logically sound: by decomposing high-dimensional image modeling into hierarchical tasks conditioned on structured, simplified representations, the model aims to reduce the burden on the primary latent space. However, the manuscript suffers from significant clarity issues at this stage. Most concerning is the leap from the abstract concept of "self-supervised representations" to the formal methodology; the mathematical integration of these non-trainable transformations into the existing VAE ELBO is not yet explicitly derived in a way that distinguishes it from conditional VAEs or standard hierarchical VAEs. Furthermore, the writing is occasionally hindered by technical jargon that is introduced without sufficient contextual definition, and the prose in the introduction—specifically regarding the trade-off in memory and data quality—demands more rigorous empirical justification before it can be accepted as a primary contribution. To strengthen the paper, the authors must provide a clearer mathematical formulation that demonstrates exactly how these "non-trainable" inputs modify the objective function without increasing the complexity of the inference process to an impractical degree.

comments.title: Severe Omissions in Methodology and Formal Definitions
comments.quote: Further, we propose to choose the following distributions: [blank space]
comments.explanation: Multiple core components of the model are entirely missing or underspecified: the functional forms for p(y|u) and p(z|u, y) (Section 3.2), the Hierarchical ELBO objective equation (Section 3.4), and the definition of 'conditional reconstruction' (Section 3.3). These gaps prevent reproduction and verification of the primary technical contributions.

comments.title: Mathematical Formalism Issues in ELBO and Jacobian Notation
comments.explanation: The paper contains critical errors in mathematical representation: OCR-corrupted Jacobian notation in Section 2.2 and an underspecified ELBO (Eq. 4) that lacks clear definitions for integration/summation bounds and variables. Furthermore, there is an underlying conceptual conflict regarding the conditioning of p(y|u) versus the inclusion of deterministic constraints in the variational objective.

comments.title: Inconsistent Variational Inference Architecture
comments.quote: Q(u, z|x, y) = q(u|y)q(z|x)
comments.explanation: The defined variational posterior q(z|x) is independent of u and y, creating a potential mismatch with the generative model p(z|u, y). The authors fail to justify how this independence assumption satisfies the marginal likelihood bound given the dependencies in the hierarchical generative path.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated Claims and Missing Visual Evidence
comments.quote: The latents learn to add extra information, and we can alter details of an image like facial expressions (Figure 12.ii).
comments.explanation: The paper makes several claims—specifically regarding latent space manipulation (facial expression changes) and superior generative capacity—that are unsupported by the provided experimental sections. These claims rely on figures (11 and 12) that are absent from the provided text, and the narrative lacks a reconciliation between the claimed performance and observed worse BPD scores.

comments.title: Grammatical and Editorial Errors
comments.quote: _bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior.
comments.explanation: The document contains broken sentence fragments (Section 4.2) and references missing figures, suggesting significant issues in the final editing or export process that impact readability.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an interesting conceptual framework, "selfVAE," which attempts to bridge self-supervised learning with variational inference by incorporating deterministic, task-specific image transformations into the generative process. The motivation is logically sound: by decomposing high-dimensional image modeling into hierarchical tasks conditioned on structured, simplified representations, the model aims to reduce the burden on the primary latent space. However, the manuscript suffers from significant clarity issues at this stage. Most concerning is the leap from the abstract concept of "self-supervised representations" to the formal methodology; the mathematical integration of these non-trainable transformations into the existing VAE ELBO is not yet explicitly derived in a way that distinguishes it from conditional VAEs or standard hierarchical VAEs. Furthermore, the writing is occasionally hindered by technical jargon that is introduced without sufficient contextual definition, and the prose in the introduction—specifically regarding the trade-off in memory and data quality—demands more rigorous empirical justification before it can be accepted as a primary contribution. To strengthen the paper, the authors must provide a clearer mathematical formulation that demonstrates exactly how these "non-trainable" inputs modify the objective function without increasing the complexity of the inference process to an impractical degree.

comments.title: OCR-corrupted Jacobian determinant notation
comments.quote: _@fi_ ( **v** _i−_ 1) _th_ where is the Jacobian-determinant of the _i_ transformation.  _@_ **v** _i−_ 1
comments.explanation: The text contains a clear OCR failure in the notation for the Jacobian determinant in Section 2.2. The sentence is disjointed ('_th_ where is'), and the Jacobian symbols are rendered as ''. While the intent (referencing the change of variables formula) is standard and clear to an expert, the mathematical expression required to make this section formal and complete is entirely missing, making it impossible for a reader to verify the derivation.

comments.title: Underspecified index notation in objective function
comments.explanation: Equation (4) describes the ELBO for the selfVAE, but the definition of the expectation operator E_Q is ambiguous. In the paragraph immediately preceding the equation, the authors define Q(u, z | x, y) = q(u | y)q(z | x). However, the summation/integral notation for the expectation is completely omitted (or OCR'd out), which is problematic because the terms within the log are conditioned on variables (z, u) that are sampled from different distributions. Without the explicit index or integration variables defined for the expectation, it is strictly underspecified.

comments.title: Implicitly defined or missing distribution formulas
comments.quote: Further, we propose to choose the following distributions:
comments.explanation: The sentence immediately following this leads into a colon, but in the provided text, the actual definitions of the distributions (e.g., p(x|y,z), p(z|u,y), etc.) are missing. Given this is the primary 'Method' section where the model architecture is defined, the absence of these core definitions makes the paper impossible to reproduce or fully evaluate.

comments.title: Unspecified Variational Distribution for q(z|x, y)
comments.quote: Q(u, z|x, y) = q(u|y)q(z|x)
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors define the variational posterior Q(u, z|x, y) as factorizing into q(u|y)q(z|x). However, the ELBO derivation in equation (4) includes the term ln p(z|u, y). This implies that the model's generative path for z depends on both u and y. If the inference model q(z|x) is strictly independent of u and y, there is a potential mismatch between the flexibility of the generative model and the inference model. Furthermore, the ELBO derivation seems to contain ln p(z|u, y) while the inference model is defined as q(z|x). Usually, to lower bound the marginal likelihood correctly, the posterior should be conditioned in a way that matches the dependencies in the likelihood. The independence of the posterior q(z|x) from u and y seems to simplify the model, but it is not explicitly justified how this satisfies the bound for p(x|y, z).

comments.title: Ambiguity in Conditional Reconstruction Definition
comments.quote: and for the latter ( conditional reconstruction ):
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the text introduces 'conditional generation' and 'conditional reconstruction', but the latter is left as an empty heading with no corresponding equation or process provided. While the reader can infer some logic from subsequent paragraphs, the missing definition for 'conditional reconstruction' in the text gap constitutes an underspecified methodology for a core contribution of the paper.

comments.title: Implicitly defined p(y|u) and p(z|u, y)
comments.quote: Further, we propose to choose the following distributions: [blank space]
comments.explanation: Section 3.2 states 'Further, we propose to choose the following distributions:' followed by a blank space (or missing block). While the text proceeds to mention the discretized logistic distribution and the flow-based prior for p(u), the specific functional forms for p(y|u) and p(z|u, y) that bridge the hierarchical gap are not provided. This makes the reproduction of the model structure difficult for an academic paper presenting a novel architecture.

comments.title: Broken sentence fragment in Section 4.2
comments.quote: _bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior.
comments.explanation: The text at the beginning of the second paragraph of section 4.2 starts with a fragment '_bpd_ scores than the VAE...' suggesting that the preceding subject/comparison was omitted during the final editing or OCR process. While the context implies a comparison of selfVAE performance, the lack of a complete sentence makes the claim about 'higher-dimensional data' grammatically incoherent.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Table 1 and Section 4.2 regarding CIFAR-10
comments.quote: The selfVAE-downscale achieves worse _bpd_ than the VAE.
comments.explanation: Looking at Table 1 for CIFAR-10, VAE (ours) has 3.51 bpd, and selfVAE-downscale has 3.65 bpd. This confirms the text's statement. However, the subsequent analysis suggests that 'the hierarchical structure... allows to encode missing information... in contrast to the vanilla VAE'. While the authors correctly note the performance drop on small images (32x32), the phrasing creates a contradiction in the narrative arc—the paper frames the selfVAE as an improvement, yet the quantitative results for the primary benchmark support the VAE over the selfVAE-downscale. The text lacks a clear reconciliation of why the 'potential' for generative modeling translates to worse log-likelihood scores on the standard benchmark.

comments.title: Missing definition of hierarchical objective function
comments.quote: that yields the following objective:
comments.explanation: In Section 3.4, after introducing the hierarchical structure of K transformations and the corresponding variational distributions, the text states 'that yields the following objective:' but does not actually provide the equation for the hierarchical ELBO objective. This leaves a significant gap in the method description, as the reader cannot see how the K-level summation is formalized following the 2-level ELBO given in equation (4).

comments.title: Reference to missing figure in text
comments.quote: In the selfVAE-sketch, we can retrieve the image with high accuracy by using only 16% of the original data, as it manages to encode all the texture of the image to $\mathbf{z}$ (Figure 11).
comments.explanation: The text references a 'Figure 11' to support the claim about encoding texture information in selfVAE-sketch. However, looking at the provided context (which goes up to Figure 6), this figure does not exist. While the previous figures (1-6) are provided, the absence of Figure 11 makes the specific claim about the performance of selfVAE-sketch less verifiable for the reader and suggests a potential mismatch between the current text and the paper's final figure set.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding reconstruction mechanism
comments.quote: Lastly, the latents learn to add extra information, which defines the end result, and we can alter details of an image like facial expressions (Figure 12.ii).
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims that the model can alter image details like facial expressions and references a 'Figure 12.ii'. Similar to the issue with Figure 11, Figure 12 is absent from the provided material. Furthermore, the paper never explicitly describes an experiment or method related to latent space manipulation (e.g., latent interpolation or steering) to support the capability of changing 'facial expressions'. The claim appears in the conclusion without having been demonstrated or adequately explained in the methodology or experimental sections.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the ELBO expression (Eq. 4)
comments.quote: ln p(x, y) ≥ E Q [ln pθ(x|y, z) + ln p(z|u, y) + ln p(y|u) + ln p(u) - ln q(z|x) - ln q(u|y)]
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors define a variational distribution Q(u, z|x, y) = q(u|y)q(z|x). Expanding the ELBO (log p(x,y) ≥ E_Q[log p(x,y,u,z) - log Q(u,z|x,y)]), we get E_Q[log p(x|y,z,u) + log p(z|y,u) + log p(y|u) + log p(u) - log q(z|x) - log q(u|y)]. Assuming p(x|y,z,u) = p(x|y,z) (x and u are conditionally independent given y,z), the terms match. However, the ELBO should theoretically include an expectation over the joint density or handle the delta constraint p(y|x) = delta(y-d(x)). The provided equation treats y as an observed variable in the generative likelihood, but the model later claims to sample y from p(y|u). If y is determined by x via delta(y-d(x)), the term log p(y|u) is problematic because y is fixed by the input. While the authors intend to decompose the density, the expression conflates the marginalization of u and z with the deterministic constraint, potentially leading to incorrect gradient flow during implementation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the generative process
comments.quote: u ~ p(u) -> y ~ p(y|u) -> z ~ p(z|u, y) -> x ~ p(x|y, z)
comments.explanation: The text states the model utilizes deterministic transformations d(x), but then defines a generative process where y is sampled from p(y|u). If y is the result of a deterministic transformation of x, it is not a stochastic variable in the standard generative sense unless p(y|u) is a learned approximation. This creates an ambiguity between the 'self-supervised representation' (which implies a fixed, non-trainable feature) and 'generative modeling' (which implies learning the distribution of that feature). It is unclear if p(y|u) is a learned model or a fixed projection. This lack of specification makes it difficult to understand how the 'unconditional generative functionality' is truly preserved if the model relies on a hard-coded d(x) at training time.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding bits-per-dimension (bpd) performance
comments.quote: The selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models. ... This result is promising and indicates that the proposed approach is of great potential for generative modelling.
comments.explanation: The authors claim superior 'generative capabilities' based on FID scores while acknowledging that their bpd scores are worse than the baseline VAE. In the literature, a trade-off is often observed, but claiming the approach is generally superior without reconciling the drop in likelihood density estimation is a potential overclaim. While they explain the bpd gap as being due to the small image size (32x32), the leap to 'great potential' for generative modeling based on FID alone (which is known to be biased toward perception over diversity/density) is a logic gap that requires more cautious framing.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting idea—incorporating deterministic, discrete transformations (e.g., downscaling, edge detection) as additional variables in a VAE framework—but its overall contribution and clarity are undermined by several significant issues. First, the term “self-supervised” is misleading: the transformations are hand-crafted and not learned from data, which contradicts the standard notion of self-supervised learning as a pretext-task-driven process. This conceptual mismatch calls the novelty into question, as the approach essentially reduces to a conditional VAE with pre-defined side information, a well-established technique in super-resolution and compression literature. Second, the description of the hierarchical architecture and how multiple transformations are integrated remains vague; the excerpt lacks a formal objective function or derivation showing how the deterministic variables interact with the ELBO. Third, the paper’s claims of improved quantitative performance are not substantiated in the provided text, and without a clear baseline comparison (e.g., against standard conditional VAEs or hierarchical VAEs), the empirical contribution is unclear. The writing quality is adequate, but the paper would benefit from a more precise framing of its contribution and a rigorous justification of why deterministic transformations constitute a “self-supervised” representation.

comments.title: Duplicate FID values for PixelCNN and GLOW in Table 1
comments.quote:
PixelCNN (van den Oord et al.,2016) 3.14 65.93
GLOW (Kingma & Dhariwal,2018) 3.35 65.93
comments.explanation: Both PixelCNN and GLOW are reported with exactly the same FID score (65.93) for CIFAR-10. This is highly improbable because GLOW is a flow-based model and typically achieves much lower FID (e.g., around 40-50 on CIFAR-10 in other works). The identical value suggests a possible typo, placeholder, or OCR duplication. Even if the number is coincidentally the same, the lack of explanation makes it suspicious. This inconsistency could mislead comparisons and weaken the credibility of the quantitative results.

comments.title: Overclaim that selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID than other generative models
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models.
comments.explanation: In Table 1, DDPM is listed with an FID of 5.24, which is far lower (better) than the selfVAE-downscale's FID of 34.71. The statement incorrectly implies that selfVAE outperforms all other generative models listed, including DDPM. The claim should be qualified (e.g., 'than other VAE-based models') or DDPM should be excluded from the comparison.

comments.title: Orphaned sentence fragment in quantitative results
comments.quote: _bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior.
comments.explanation: This phrase appears as a standalone line after the discussion of FID scores. It lacks a subject and a verb, making it ungrammatical and confusing. progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting idea—incorporating deterministic, discrete transformations (e.g., downscaling, edge detection) as additional variables in a VAE framework—but its overall contribution and clarity are undermined by several significant issues. First, the term “self-supervised” is misleading: the transformations are hand-crafted and not learned from data, which contradicts the standard notion of self-supervised learning as a pretext-task-driven process. This conceptual mismatch calls the novelty into question, as the approach essentially reduces to a conditional VAE with pre-defined side information, a well-established technique in super-resolution and compression literature. Second, the description of the hierarchical architecture and how multiple transformations are integrated remains vague; the excerpt lacks a formal objective function or derivation showing how the deterministic variables interact with the ELBO. Third, the paper’s claims of improved quantitative performance are not substantiated in the provided text, and without a clear baseline comparison (e.g., against standard conditional VAEs or hierarchical VAEs), the empirical contribution is unclear. The writing quality is adequate, but the paper would benefit from a more precise framing of its contribution and a rigorous justification of why deterministic transformations constitute a “self-supervised” representation.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper proposes **selfVAE**, a VAE framework that incorporates deterministic transformations (e.g., downscaling, edge detection) as latent variables to enable conditional generation and facilitate data compression tasks. While the motivation to exploit multi-scale structure through a hierarchical architecture is sound and potentially beneficial for modeling complex distributions, the manuscript currently suffers from significant conceptual, technical, and presentation issues. Most notably, the use of the term **"self-supervised representations"** for fixed, deterministic operations like downscaling is highly misleading and conflates this approach with the distinct self-supervised learning paradigm (e.g., contrastive or pretext-task learning), which risks confusing the community and mischaracterizing the novelty; the authors should adopt a more precise term such as "deterministic latent variables" or "structured representations." Furthermore, the distinction between this method and well-established **multi-scale VAEs** and discrete latent models is not sufficiently articulated, and the contribution relative to strong baselines needs to be rigorously validated. Technically, the description of using "deterministic" variables as "latents" is ambiguous: if the transformation is a fixed function of the input, the role of the learned variational posterior is unclear, and the handling of non-differentiable discrete operations in the training objective requires explicit clarification. Finally, the abstract begins with a stray word ("intelligence."), indicating a lack of proofreading that undermines the paper's polish. The authors should redefine the terminology, clarify the mathematical interaction between the deterministic transformations and the probabilistic latent space, and strengthen the experimental comparison against modern multi-scale VAE variants.

comments.title: Broken sentence obscures model comparison in Sec 4.2
comments.quote: **_bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior.** This indicates the benefit of employing a multileveled self-supervised framework against the VAE in higher-dimensional data, where the plain model fails to scale efficiently.
comments.explanation: The paragraph begins with a mangled sentence fragment that lacks a subject and verb. Based on Table 1 and the surrounding context, it is clear the authors intend to state that the hierarchical model (selfVAE-downscale-3lvl) achieves significantly lower (better) bpd scores than the standard VAE. However, as written, the claim about which model outperforms the baseline is unreadable. This appears to be an OCR or copy-paste error that disrupts the logical flow of the results analysis.

comments.title: Overstated claim regarding FID performance
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models.
comments.explanation: According to Table 1, the selfVAE-downscale achieves an FID of 34.71, whereas DDPM (a standard benchmark in the table) achieves an FID of 5.24. The claim that selfVAE-downscale performs better than 'the other generative models' is misleading without qualification. It is only better than the authors' own VAE baseline and some older models listed, but trails recent diffusion-based methods significantly. The authors should temper this claim to specify that selfVAE outperforms traditional VAE-based baselines or models with comparable parameter counts.

comments.title: Abrupt transition from marginal to joint modeling objective
comments.quote: However, since we are interested in decomposing the problem of modeling a complex distribution p(x), we propose to model p(x|y) p(y) instead, and utilize the variational inference of the form Q(u, z|x, y) = q(u|y) q(z|x) that yields:
comments.explanation: The authors state their goal is to model $p(\mathbf{x})$, but immediately pivot to modeling the joint distribution $p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ via the proposed ELBO. While it is standard practice in auxiliary variable models to optimize a bound on the joint likelihood and recover the marginal via integration, the transition is abrupt. Furthermore, because $\mathbf{y}$ is generated by a deterministic downsampling transformation $d(\cdot)$, the joint distribution $p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y})$ is supported on a lower-dimensional manifold. The authors should briefly clarify how the joint ELBO relates to the marginal likelihood $p(\mathbf{x})$ and whether the deterministic map is treated as a fixed conditioning variable during decoding or as a random variable integrated out during generation.

comments.title: Lack of hyperparameter details for data augmentation
comments.quote: We augment the training data by using random horizontal flips and random affine transformations and normalize the data uniformly in the range (0, 1).
comments.explanation: The specific parameters for 'random affine transformations' (e.g., maximum rotation angle, shear limits, translation bounds) are not specified in the main text. While these details are often relegated to appendices in ICLR submissions, providing them here or explicitly referencing a supplementary file is important for reproducibility, especially when claiming competitive performance on benchmarks. The omission leaves the exact experimental setup slightly underspecified for independent verification.

Upon checking the surrounding context and standard literature on flow-based priors, I recognized this as a corrupted rendering of the log-Jacobian determinant term, likely \(\log \left| \det \frac{\partial f_i(\mathbf{v}_{i-1})}{\partial \mathbf{v}_{i-1}} \right|\). The Kronecker delta applies strictly to discrete domains, whereas the Dirac delta function is the standard convention for deterministic mappings over continuous domains. This is a minor terminological inaccuracy that could confuse readers familiar with probability theory conventions, though it does not alter the mathematical derivation.

comments.title: Questionable claim regarding FID score comparisons
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models.
comments.explanation: The authors claim selfVAE-downscale has 'significantly better FID scores than the other generative models'. Table 1 shows selfVAE-downscale achieving an FID of 34.71, while DDPM achieves 5.24 (and 3.17 on training). DDPM is listed among the 'other generative models' in the table, making the claim factually inaccurate as written. The authors likely meant to restrict the comparison to VAE-based architectures or specific baselines, but the phrasing overstates the result and could mislead readers about the model's comparative standing.

comments.title: Fragmented sentence in quantitative results
comments.quote: _bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior. This indicates the benefit of employing a multileveled self-supervised framework against the VAE in higher-dimensional data, where the plain model fails to scale efficiently.
comments.explanation: The paragraph begins with a sentence fragment '_bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior.', missing its subject and likely the comparative adjective (e.g., 'lower' or 'improved'). Based on the preceding discussion and Table 1 data (selfVAE-downscale-3lvl: 2.88 bpd vs. VAE: 3.12 bpd), the intended meaning is recoverable. This appears to be a layout/OCR truncation rather than a conceptual error, but it disrupts the logical flow of the results analysis.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of bpd improvements in conclusion
comments.quote: The experimental results seem to confirm that hierarchical architectures perform better and allow to obtain both better _bpd_ scores and better generations and reconstructions.
comments.explanation: I initially questioned the blanket claim that the experimental results confirm 'better bpd scores' for the proposed architectures. Checking Table 1 and Section 4.2, I noted that the single-level `selfVAE-downscale` on CIFAR-10 actually yields a higher (worse) bpd of 3.65 compared to the baseline VAE's 3.51. The authors correctly acknowledge this limitation in the main text, attributing it to the small image size where hierarchical decomposition offers little advantage. The bpd improvements are indeed consistent for the hierarchical 3-level models on the larger CelebA and Imagenette64 datasets (2.88 vs 3.12 and 3.70 vs 3.85, respectively). While the conclusion correctly specifies 'hierarchical architectures,' the phrasing glosses over the single-level case where bpd degrades, which could mislead readers into expecting consistent bpd improvements across all proposed variants. The authors accurately capture the qualitative generation and reconstruction improvements, but the bpd claim requires clearer scope limitation in the summary. This is a moderate issue as it pertains to precise result summarization and could affect how readers interpret the model's density estimation capabilities.

comments.title: Overclaim regarding FID performance in Section 4.2
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than 'the other generative models'. However, Table 1 explicitly lists DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) with an FID of 5.24, which is substantially better than the selfVAE-downscale's FID of 34.71. This statement directly contradicts the provided table and misrepresents the model's comparative standing. The authors likely intended to say 'better than the VAE baseline and earlier baselines', but as written, it is misleading and undermines the credibility of the quantitative comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'reconstruction' for generative sampling paths in Section 3.3
comments.quote: In this way, we can reconstruct an image in two ways, namely, _reconstruction 1_ : ... and _reconstruction 2_ :
comments.explanation: Both proposed methods involve sampling latent variables ($\mathbf{y} \sim p(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{u})$ and $\mathbf{z} \sim p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{y})$ or $\mathbf{z} \sim q(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_{[*]})$) rather than performing a deterministic inversion or standard VAE reconstruction of the input $\mathbf{x}_{[*]}$. While the authors clarify immediately after that these are intended for compression trade-offs ('each option creates a different ratio of the reconstruction quality against the memory'), labeling fully generative sampling paths as 'reconstruction' could initially mislead a reader expecting an exact or approximate inversion of the input image. The terminology should be clarified to distinguish between conditional generation and reconstruction for the stated compression use case.

comments.title: Underspecified variational factorization choice in Section 3.2
comments.quote: we propose to model _p_ ( **x** _|_ **y** ) _p_ ( **y** ) instead, and utilize the variational inference of the form _Q_ ( **u** _,_ **z** _|_ **x** _,_ **y** ) = _q_ ( **u** _|_ **y** ) _q_ ( **z** _|_ **x** ) that yields:
comments.explanation: The factorization $Q(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = q(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{y})q(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})$ is introduced as a given without justification. In variational inference, the choice of factorization directly impacts the tightness of the ELBO and the model's expressivity. A careful reader might question why $\mathbf{u}$ is not conditioned on $\mathbf{x}$ or why $\mathbf{z}$ is not conditioned on $\mathbf{y}$, as tighter bounds or better performance could potentially be achieved with more expressive posteriors. Stating this architectural choice without deriving it or discussing its implications for the bound leaves a key methodological detail underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent dataset split description in Section 4.1
comments.quote: _CIFAR-10_ The CIFAR-10 dataset is a well-known image benchmark data containing 60.000 training examples and 10.000 validation examples. From the training data, we put aside 15% randomly selected images as the test set.
comments.explanation: The description of the CIFAR-10 splits is internally contradictory and diverges from the standard benchmark. Standard CIFAR-10 has 50,000 training and 10,000 test images. The text first mentions 60,000 training and 10,000 validation examples, then states that 15% of the *training* data is set aside as the *test* set. This phrasing is confusing regarding whether the 10,000 'validation' examples serve as the test set, or if the 15% split overrides it. While redefining splits is common, the lack of clarity here could mislead readers trying to reproduce the experiments or compare against standard benchmarks.

The Kronecker delta applies strictly to discrete domains, whereas the Dirac delta function is the standard convention for deterministic mappings over continuous domains. This is a minor terminological inaccuracy that could confuse readers familiar with probability theory conventions, though it does not alter the mathematical derivation.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulation for conditional reconstruction in Section 3.3
comments.quote:
and for the latter ( _conditional reconstruction_ ):

If **y** is a downscaling transformation of the input image...
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states it will describe a generative process for 'conditional reconstruction' but immediately transitions to a new paragraph discussing super-resolution applications without providing the corresponding equation or algorithm steps. Given the paper's emphasis on detailing multiple sampling and reconstruction pathways (generation, conditional generation, reconstruction 1, reconstruction 2), this omission leaves a key methodological component completely unspecified. A careful reader cannot verify the exact inference/generation flow for this case without guessing, which significantly impacts reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing variational distributions and objective equations in Section 3.4
comments.quote:
We take _K_ self-supervised data transformations _dk_ ( _·_ ) that give _K_ representations denoted by **y** 1: _K_ = [ **y** 1 _, . . . ,_ **y** _K_ ], and the following variational distributions:

**----- Start of picture text -----**
... (**----- End of picture text -----**)

that yields the following objective:

## 4 EXPERIMENTS
comments.explanation: In the introduction to the Hierarchical Self-Supervised VAE, the text promises to define the variational distributions for the \(K\)-level architecture and present the resulting objective function. However, the PDF extraction has replaced these equations with diagram labels from Figure 3. Without the explicit factorization of the hierarchical encoder/decoder and the full ELBO expression, the generalization from the 2-level to the \(K\)-level model is mathematically underspecified. While the motivation is clear, the absence of the core equations makes it impossible to assess the exact training procedure or compare against standard hierarchical VAE formulations.

comments.title: Fragmented paragraph opening in Section 4.2
comments.quote: _bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior. This indicates the benefit...
comments.explanation: The first paragraph of Section 4.2 begins mid-sentence, missing its subject and initial clause. I checked Table 1 and the surrounding text to reconstruct the context, which strongly suggests the sentence was discussing how the hierarchical selfVAE achieves lower BPD scores than the baseline VAE. While this is likely a harmless two-column layout parsing artifact, starting a results analysis paragraph with a fragment disrupts the logical flow. A reader not cross-referencing the table immediately might experience momentary confusion regarding the subject of the claim.

comments.title: Missing conditional reconstruction sampling chain
comments.quote:
and for the latter ( conditional reconstruction ):

If **y** is a downscaling transformation of the input image, selfVAE can be used in a manner similar to the super-resolution (Gatopoulos et al., 2020).
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly introduce 'conditional reconstruction' as a distinct generative/inferential pathway, and provide explicit sampling chains for 'conditional generation', 'reconstruction 1', and 'reconstruction 2'. However, the corresponding chain for 'conditional reconstruction' is completely absent. I initially suspected a simple OCR drop, but even assuming the authors intended a standard hierarchical chain (e.g., y* → u~q → y~p → z~p → x~p), its absence leaves the exact procedure undefined. A careful reader cannot verify how variables are conditioned or sampled without this formula. Given the methodological focus on multiple reconstruction pathways, this gap is notable.

comments.title: Missing variational distributions in model formulation
comments.quote:
Further, we propose to choose the following distributions:

where Dlogistic is defined as the discretized logistic distribution (Salimans et al., 2017), and we utilize a flow-based model for pλ(u).
comments.explanation: Section 3.2 states that specific distributions will be chosen but the equations are entirely missing, likely due to PDF column-layout issues during OCR extraction. The text references the discretized logistic distribution and a flow-based prior for u, but omits the encoder parameterizations qφ(z|x) and qψ(u|y), as well as the decoder pθ(x|y,z). While the context strongly implies standard VAE-style parameterizations (e.g., mean/scale networks outputting logistic parameters), the omission technically renders the model underspecified for strict reproducibility. I checked the surrounding text and standard conventions, which suggest this is a layout artifact, but the absence still hampers full technical verification.

comments.title: Missing hierarchical ELBO objective
comments.quote:
that yields the following objective:

## 4 EXPERIMENTS
comments.explanation: Section 3.4 introduces a K-level hierarchical selfVAE and states it 'yields the following objective', but the generalized ELBO equation is completely missing. For hierarchical VAEs, deriving the correct factorization and variational bound across K levels is non-trivial and essential for understanding the method. Like the previous issues, this is almost certainly a PDF extraction artifact. However, without the explicit objective, readers cannot verify how the hierarchical dependencies are handled or how the bound extends Eq. (4).

comments.title: Questionable claim regarding FID score comparisons
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models.
comments.explanation: The authors claim selfVAE-downscale has 'significantly better FID scores than the other generative models'. Table 1 shows selfVAE-downscale achieving an FID of 34.71, while DDPM achieves 5.24 (and 3.17 on training). DDPM is listed among the 'other generative models' in the table, making the claim factually inaccurate as written. The authors likely meant to restrict the comparison to VAE-based architectures or specific baselines, but the phrasing overstates the result and could mislead readers about the model's comparative standing.

comments.title: Fragmented sentence in quantitative results
comments.quote: _bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior. This indicates the benefit of employing a multileveled self-supervised framework against the VAE in higher-dimensional data, where the plain model fails to scale efficiently.
comments.explanation: The paragraph begins with a sentence fragment '_bpd_ scores than the VAE with the bijective prior.', missing its subject and likely the comparative adjective (e.g., 'lower' or 'improved'). Based on the preceding discussion and Table 1 data (selfVAE-downscale-3lvl: 2.88 bpd vs. VAE: 3.12 bpd), the intended meaning is recoverable. This appears to be a layout/OCR truncation rather than a conceptual error, but it disrupts the logical flow of the results analysis.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of bpd improvements in conclusion
comments.quote: The experimental results seem to confirm that hierarchical architectures perform better and allow to obtain both better _bpd_ scores and better generations and reconstructions.
comments.explanation: I initially questioned the blanket claim that the experimental results confirm 'better bpd scores' for the proposed architectures. Checking Table 1 and Section 4.2, I noted that the single-level `selfVAE-downscale` on CIFAR-10 actually yields a higher (worse) bpd of 3.65 compared to the baseline VAE's 3.51. The authors correctly acknowledge this limitation in the main text, attributing it to the small image size where hierarchical decomposition offers little advantage. The bpd improvements are indeed consistent for the hierarchical 3-level models on the larger CelebA and Imagenette64 datasets (2.88 vs 3.12 and 3.70 vs 3.85, respectively). While the conclusion correctly specifies 'hierarchical architectures,' the phrasing glosses over the single-level case where bpd degrades, which could mislead readers into expecting consistent bpd improvements across all proposed variants. The authors accurately capture the qualitative generation and reconstruction improvements, but the bpd claim requires clearer scope limitation in the summary. This is a moderate issue as it pertains to precise result summarization and could affect how readers interpret the model's density estimation capabilities.

comments.title: Overclaim regarding FID performance in Section 4.2
comments.quote: Nevertheless, the selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than the other generative models.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that selfVAE-downscale achieves significantly better FID scores than 'the other generative models'. However, Table 1 explicitly lists DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) with an FID of 5.24, which is substantially better than the selfVAE-downscale's FID of 34.71. This statement directly contradicts the provided table and misrepresents the model's comparative standing. The authors likely intended to say 'better than the VAE baseline and earlier baselines', but as written, it is misleading and undermines the credibility of the quantitative comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'reconstruction' for generative sampling paths in Section 3.3
comments.quote: In this way, we can reconstruct an image in two ways, namely, _reconstruction 1_ : ... and _reconstruction 2_ :
comments.explanation: Both proposed methods involve sampling latent variables ($\mathbf{y} \sim p(\mathbf{y}|\mathbf{u})$ and $\mathbf{z} \sim p(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{y})$ or $\mathbf{z} \sim q(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}_{[*]})$) rather than performing a deterministic inversion or standard VAE reconstruction of the input $\mathbf{x}_{[*]}$. While the authors clarify immediately after that these are intended for compression trade-offs ('each option creates a different ratio of the reconstruction quality against the memory'), labeling fully generative sampling paths as 'reconstruction' could initially mislead a reader expecting an exact or approximate inversion of the input image. The terminology should be clarified to distinguish between conditional generation and reconstruction for the stated compression use case.

comments.title: Underspecified variational factorization choice in Section 3.2
comments.quote: we propose to model _p_ ( **x** _|_ **y** ) _p_ ( **y** ) instead, and utilize the variational inference of the form _Q_ ( **u** _,_ **z** _|_ **x** _,_ **y** ) = _q_ ( **u** _|_ **y** ) _q_ ( **z** _|_ **x** ) that yields:
comments.explanation: The factorization $Q(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = q(\mathbf{u}|\mathbf{y})q(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})$ is introduced as a given without justification. In variational inference, the choice of factorization directly impacts the tightness of the ELBO and the model's expressivity. A careful reader might question why $\mathbf{u}$ is not conditioned on $\mathbf{x}$ or why $\mathbf{z}$ is not conditioned on $\mathbf{y}$, as tighter bounds or better performance could potentially be achieved with more expressive posteriors. Stating this architectural choice without deriving it or discussing its implications for the bound leaves a key methodological detail underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent dataset split description in Section 4.1
comments.quote: _CIFAR-10_ The CIFAR-10 dataset is a well-known image benchmark data containing 60.000 training examples and 10.000 validation examples. From the training data, we put aside 15% randomly selected images as the test set.
comments.explanation: The description of the CIFAR-10 splits is internally contradictory and diverges from the standard benchmark. Standard CIFAR-10 has 50,000 training and 10,000 test images. The text first mentions 60,000 training and 10,000 validation examples, then states that 15% of the *training* data is set aside as the *test* set. This phrasing is confusing regarding whether the 10,000 'validation' examples serve as the test set, or if the 15% split overrides it. While redefining splits is common, the lack of clarity here could mislead readers trying to reproduce the experiments or compare against standard benchmarks.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper proposes a novel extension to Variational Auto-Encoders (selfVAEs) by incorporating deterministic, discrete transformations of data (e.g., downscaling, edge detection) as latent variables. The motivation to leverage partial, non-trainable information to assist the model in learning complex distributions is compelling and addresses common VAE weaknesses such as over-regularization and density estimation challenges. The preliminary writing is clear, and the potential for trade-offs between memory and quality in data compression is an attractive practical contribution. However, the most significant issue currently arising is the lack of formal technical details regarding the handling of discrete latent variables. Specifically, the text does not yet explain how gradients are estimated or how the posterior $q_\phi(z|x)$ is constructed for these discrete, non-differentiable transformations; without explicit discussion of the inference mechanism (e.g., straight-through estimation or auxiliary networks), the validity of the proposed objective function and training procedure remains unclear.

comments.title: Incorrect VAE ELBO in Background Section
comments.explanation: The standard Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) for a Variational Auto-Encoder (VAE) is derived as \( \ln p(x) \geq \mathbb{E}_{q\phi(z|x)}[\ln p_\theta(x|z) + \ln p(z) - \ln q_\phi(z|x)] \). The provided equation contains two \( - \ln q(z|x) \) terms, which is mathematically incorrect. It should contain only one negative log-likelihood term for the variational posterior.

comments.title: Ambiguous Claim About Memory and Sampling
comments.quote: However, every inferred variable needs to be sent, thus, more sampling corresponds to lower memory requirements.
comments.explanation: The sentence claims that 'more sampling' leads to 'lower memory requirements'. This is logically confusing. In the context of transmission and data compression, 'sampling' typically refers to generating discrete symbols (e.g., from a discrete probability distribution like a sketch or bitstream). While sending these discrete symbols is memory-efficient compared to sending full float-valued latent variables (like $z$ or $u$), the causal link described is flawed: the reduction in memory comes from using *discrete* representations, not from the act of *sampling* itself. Moreover, sending sampled values still requires encoding the sample itself. The statement could mislead readers into thinking that random sampling operations (e.g., stochastic gradients) are beneficial for memory efficiency in transmission, whereas it likely refers to the communication of discrete codebook indices.

comments.title: Ambiguous Test Set Partitioning
comments.quote: The CIFAR-10 dataset is a well-known image benchmark data containing 60.000 training examples and 10.000 validation examples. From the training data, we put aside 15% randomly selected images as the test set.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states 60,000 training examples and then mentions 10,000 validation examples. Later, it says 15% of the training data is set aside as a test set. This creates ambiguity: does the 10,000 validation examples refer to a hold-out set separate from the 60,000 training examples (implying 70,000 total), or is it part of the original 60,000 training split? Furthermore, taking 15% (9,000 images) *from* the training set implies the final training set would be 51,000. It is unclear how the original 10,000 validation examples are handled in conjunction with this new 9,000 test set. This makes the training/validation/test split non-reproducible.

comments.title: Inconsistent Image Cropping Description
comments.quote: We cropped original images on the 40 vertical and 15 horizontal component of the crop box, which height and width were cropped to 148.
comments.explanation: The standard CelebA dataset consists of images typically cropped to 178×218 pixels. If one crops 40 pixels from the vertical dimension (218 - 40 = 178), the resulting height should be 178, not 148. Similarly, cropping 15 pixels from the horizontal dimension (178 - 15 = 163) does not result in 148. The stated cropping parameters (40 vertical, 15 horizontal) do not mathematically yield the stated resulting dimension (148). This discrepancy makes the experimental setup difficult to reproduce or verify.

comments.title: Unsupported Comparison to RealNVP
comments.quote: selfVAE-downscale-3lvl ... achieves better bpd scores than the VAE with the bijective prior. This indicates the benefit... It seems that the hierarchical structure... This result seems to be in line with the FID scores.
comments.explanation: In the provided Table 1, the 'Imagenette64' row lists 'VAE (ours)' at 3.85 and 'selfVAE-downscale-3lvl' at 3.70. However, it does not list a 'RealNVP' or 'VAE with the bijective prior' entry for this specific dataset. The text explicitly makes a comparison ('than the VAE with the bijective prior') that is not supported by the data presented in the table. Additionally, the text claims this result is 'in line with the FID scores,' but FID scores are not reported for Imagenette64 in Table 1.

comments.title: Vague Bijective Prior Justification
comments.quote: All models were employed with the bijective prior (RealNVP) comparable in terms of the number of parameters (the range of the weights of all models was from 32M to 42M).
comments.explanation: Stating that models are 'comparable in terms of the number of parameters' is vague and subjective without specifying what that comparable number is (e.g., 'with 3 layers of invertible affine couplings'). Furthermore, the mention of the 'range of the weights' (32M to 42M) is confusing; weight magnitudes depend heavily on initialization and the optimizer used (AdaMax here) and do not describe the model architecture size or capacity in a meaningful way.

comments.title: Figure Description Contains OCR Noise
comments.quote: Reconstruction 1 Reconstruction 2 Conditional  Generation ReconstructionConditional Self-Supervised Representation Self-Supervised Reconstruction
comments.explanation: The description of Figure 6 contains concatenated and garbled text (likely from poor OCR extraction of labels). Terms like 'ReconstructionConditional' appear to be a jumble of labels 'Reconstruction 3' or 'Conditional Generation'. This makes the interpretation of the figure nearly impossible from the text alone.

comments.title: Typo in concluding paragraph
comments.quote: without loosing its unconditional generative functionality.
comments.explanation: The text contains the word 'loosing', which is a spelling error for 'losing'. The context makes it clear that the intended meaning is that the model maintains its generative capabilities. This is a straightforward typographical error.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding transformations
comments.quote: However, there is a vast of possible other transformations (see Figure 2), and we leave investigating them for future work.
comments.explanation: While the paper explores deterministic transformations like downscaling and sketching, using the hyperbolic term 'vast' to describe the variety of transformations, and then immediately citing 'Figure 2' which contains only a handful (10) of specific illustrative examples, creates a slight logical gap. A reader might expect Figure 2 to visually represent a 'vast' number of cases, which it does not. The phrase 'a variety of' or 'many other' would be more precise.
